coredata.dc:title: pH-dependent selective ion exchange based on (ethylenediamintetraacetic acid-nickel)-layered double hydroxide to catalyze the polymerization of aniline for detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Sheng
Sun, Jun
Li, Yana
Xia, Dasha
Qi, Tong
Liu, Kai
Deng, Huimin
Shen, Wei
Lee, Hian Kee
coredata.dc:description: A pH-dependent selective ion exchange coupled with catalytic polymerization of aniline has been developed for sensitive detection of copper (Cu2+) and ferric ions (Fe3+). Ethylenediamintetraacetic acid (EDTA) chelated with nickel ion (Ni2+) were intercalated in a layered double hydroxide via a co-precipitation reaction. The product was subsequently applied as sorbent for the enrichment of Cu2+ at pH 6.5 and Fe3+ at pH 4.5. Since both Cu2+ and Fe3+ have stronger complex formation constants with EDTA, Ni2+ exchanges with Cu2+/Fe3+ selectively. The resulting sorbent containing Cu2+/Fe3+ was transferred to catalyze the aniline polymerization reaction, since Cu2+/Fe3+ could be released by the sorbent effectively at different pH values and have high catalytic abilities for the polymerization reaction. The resulting polyaniline with different colors were produced at different pH values, an observation that was utilized to distinguish between the colorimetric signals of Cu2+ and Fe3+. The extraction temperature, extraction time, catalysis time and pH were optimized. The results showed that this method provided low limits of detection of 0.1 nM (6.4 ng/L) for Cu2+, 1 nM (56 ng/L) for Fe3+, wide linear ranges (0.0005–2.5 µM, and 0.005–5 µM, respectively), and good linearities (r2 values of 0.9904, and 0.9965, respectively). The optimized method was applied to river water samples. Using Cu2+/Fe3+ as examples, this work provided a new and interesting approach for the convenient and efficient detection of metal ions in aqueous samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: (EDTA-Ni)-LDH
Polymerization of aniline
Copper ion
Ferric ion
pH-dependent ion exchange

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Method evaluation and genuine sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of selective extraction-release-catalysis detection process.
XRD patterns of NO3-LDH and (EDTA-Ni)-LDH; (Insert) TEM image of (EDTA-Ni)-LDH.
Effect of pH on polymerization. Sample was spiked with 200 nM Cu2+. Conditions: extraction time, 15 min; temperature, 25 °C; reaction time, 5 min; (Insert) SEM image of polyaniline when pH is at 4.5.

Effect of pH on polymerization. Sample was spiked with 200 nM Fe3+. Conditions: extraction time, 15 min; temperature, 25 °C; reaction time, 15 min; (Insert) SEM image of polyaniline when pH is at 6.5.
Effect of reaction time. Sample was spiked with 20 nM metal ions. Conditions were adopted as above mentioned optimized parameters (Sections 3.2–3.4).

UV–vis intensity (Δabsorbance) of polymerization of aniline reaction: (1) Cu2+/Fe3+ standard solution (without extraction) and (2) (EDTA-Cu2+/Fe3+)-LDH ((EDTA-Ni)-LDH after extraction of Cu2+/Fe3+ standard solution). Amount of metal ions (1 mL, 200 nM).

Absorbance of polymerization of aniline, catalyzed by sorbent after extraction of metal cations (10 μM, except Cu2+ (1 μM)) in the absence (white bar) or presence (shaded bar) of additional 1 μM Cu2+ under the most favorable conditions.

Absorbance of polymerization of aniline, which was catalyzed by sorbent after extraction of metal cations (10 μM, except Fe3+ (1 μM)) in the absence (white bar) or presence (shaded bar) of additional 1 μM Fe3+ under the most favorable conditions.

UV–vis spectra of polymerization of aniline catalyzed by (EDTA-Ni)-LDH after extraction of 0.5, 1, 10, 100, 200, 400, 1000, 2500 nM of Cu2+ standard solutions. The most favorable conditions were applied in the extraction, and catalytic reaction. Detection wavelength, 370 nm; (Insert) Linear fitting curve of log (Absorbance) versus a log (concentration of Cu2+ (nM)).

UV–vis spectra of polymerization of aniline catalyzed by (EDTA-Ni)-LDH after extraction of 5, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 5000 nM of Fe3+ standard solutions. The most favorable conditions were applied in the extraction, and catalytic reaction. Detection wavelength, 310 nm; (Insert) Linear fitting curve of log (Absorbance) versus a log (concentration of Fe3+ (nM)).

row.entry: 555 nm (UV–vis) for Cu2+; 587 nm (fluorescence) for Fe3+
410 nm (UV–vis) for Cu2+; 440 nm (fluorescence) for Fe3+
Dual-response fluorescent probe based on quinoline and benzimidazole groups
Acrylic polymers with pendant terpyridine motifs
576 nm for Fe3+, 450 nm for Cu2+
545 nm for Fe3+, 552 nm for Cu2+
1-(2-pyridinylazo)-2-naphthaleno and bromothymol blue functionalized ionic microgels (different types were prepared)
Different, based on a series of sensors
370 nm for Cu2+; 310 nm for Fe3+, both are UV–vis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in genuine and spiked river water samples.

head.ce:title: pH-dependent selective ion exchange based on (ethylenediamintetraacetic acid-nickel)-layered double hydroxide to catalyze the polymerization of aniline for detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, National University of Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A pH-dependent selective ion exchange coupled with catalytic polymerization of aniline has been developed for sensitive detection of copper (Cu2+) and ferric ions (Fe3+). Ethylenediamintetraacetic acid (EDTA) chelated with nickel ion (Ni2+) were intercalated in a layered double hydroxide via a co-precipitation reaction. The product was subsequently applied as sorbent for the enrichment of Cu2+ at pH 6.5 and Fe3+ at pH 4.5. Since both Cu2+ and Fe3+ have stronger complex formation constants with EDTA, Ni2+ exchanges with Cu2+/Fe3+ selectively. The resulting sorbent containing Cu2+/Fe3+ was transferred to catalyze the aniline polymerization reaction, since Cu2+/Fe3+ could be released by the sorbent effectively at different pH values and have high catalytic abilities for the polymerization reaction. The resulting polyaniline with different colors were produced at different pH values, an observation that was utilized to distinguish between the colorimetric signals of Cu2+ and Fe3+. The extraction temperature, extraction time, catalysis time and pH were optimized. The results showed that this method provided low limits of detection of 0.1 nM (6.4 ng/L) for Cu2+, 1 nM (56 ng/L) for Fe3+, wide linear ranges (0.0005–2.5 µM, and 0.005–5 µM, respectively), and good linearities (r2 values of 0.9904, and 0.9965, respectively). The optimized method was applied to river water samples. Using Cu2+/Fe3+ as examples, this work provided a new and interesting approach for the convenient and efficient detection of metal ions in aqueous samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Both copper ion (Cu2+) and ferric ion (Fe3+) are important nutrients for human health and play essential roles in many physiological processes (e.g. electron transport of mitochondria, dismutation of the superoxide, oxygen transport, DNA and RNA synthesis) [1–4]. However, it has been proven that high concentrations of these metal ions are toxic to human health. For example, Cu2+ at high level could lead Wilson's disease, Alzheimer's disease and Menkes syndrome [5]. Abnormality of liver and kidney function could be caused by a high concentration of Fe3+ [6]. Thus, the World Health Organization has set the maximum permissible amount of Cu2+ in drinking water at 2.0 mg/L and a suggested value of Fe3+ at 0.3 mg/L [7].

To monitor these two types of metal ions, many detection methods have been developed, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [8,9] and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [10]. However, these methods usually require expensive instrumentation and sophisticated sample preparation procedures, which limit their wider applicability. In recent years, many novel materials have been developed for their application as electrochemical, luminometric or fluorometric sensors [11–16]. For electrochemistry, ion-selective electrodes need only simple instrumentation, but can provide low limits of detection (LODs) compared to AAS or ICP-MS. However, for each metal ion a specific electrode is required and the throughput is low. Fluorometric methods have many advantages such as high sensitivity and fast analysis. Nevertheless, different metal ions require specific fluoroionophores, which usually require long synthetic routes. Meanwhile, these fluorometric materials suffer from some shortcomings, including being easily oxidized, photobleaching and toxicity [12]. For these material-based methods, the selectivities to various metal ions usually rely on either (1) the different signals produced by various sensors, which means a series of materials needs to be prepared, or detection instrumentation has to be readjusted, or replaced by another for a separate analyte; or (2) the same signals (i.e., at the same wavelength) produced under different conditions, which usually requires an additional reaction to ensure the detection is for a specific metal ion, and not for the other. Even so, when an extra reaction is conducted, it cannot completely eliminate interferences between the ions. For faster and more efficient determination of different types of metal ions, a method based on a single material which can produce different signals for various ions would be desirable.

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs) are a class of two-dimensional nanostructured materials containing exchangeable interlayer anions. Various types of anions (organic or inorganic) can intercalate into the LDH, such as ethylenediaminetetraacetatic acid (EDTA), nitriloacetic acid (NTA) and MoS4 2- [17–20]. The resulting new intercalated LDHs, for example, EDTA-LDH or MoS4 2--LDH in the above examples, can be utilized as sorbents to remove various metal ions from aqueous solution [19–22]. However, the extraction approach is not selective [23]. Previously, we reported the application of a novel material, (EDTA-Ni)-LDH, as a selective sorbent to extract Cu2+ in water [17]. The EDTA-Ni complex was prepared and intercalated into the LDH. In contrast to EDTA, EDTA-Ni showed selectivity towards Cu2+ upon pH adjustment. Analogously, we synthesized (NTA-Ni) to extract Fe3+ from aqueous samples [18]. In both cases, the captured metal ions (Cu2+ or Fe3+) were released selectively to react with colorimetric substances for their determination. These two methods showed high selectivity and high sensitivity [18]. However, like many other colorimetric, spectrophotometric, and fluorometric methods, under the same approach, one type of material can only be used to detect one specific metal ion, which limits the applicability of the material and the method itself.

Polyaniline (PANI) is an important conducting polymer that has been extensively studied due to its electrochemical, electronic and optical properties. The applications of PANI include its use in energy-storage devices, and biosensors, and as an antistatic coating, etc. [24]. In analytical chemistry, PANI has usually been employed as an electrochemistry material [25,26]. Interestingly, PANI shows a special optical property, which depends on the pH of the polymerization process. Liu et al. [27] reported that the synthesized PANI exhibited various structures which were related to the applied pH conditions. At a lower pH, a benzenoid-quinoid (head-to-tail) reaction dominated the polymerization, and the resulting PANI had more linear chains. In contrast, at a higher pH (> 5), ortho- and para-substituted carbon-carbon and carbon-nitrogen bond structures were observed, leading to more ring structures being formed. Different PANI structures present different UV absorption properties. This special optical property can be utilized to indicate pH-dependent reactions, but has been rarely applied in analytical methods.

pH-sensitive methods have been developed for detection of some metal ions [28–30]. By changing pH, the detection signal can be turned on/off easily. However, in these cases, the change of pH only affected the intensity of signals, not their shifts. Thus, these processes did not exhibit selectivities to two different metal ions. Additionally, detection methods using metal ions as catalysts in dye formation have been reported [31–33]. These formation processes are similar to the polymerization of aniline and significantly amplify the detection signals. Unlike polyaniline, these dyes usually exhibited an invariable colorimetric/fluorescent property, which limited their selectivity to more metal ions under the same detection process (usually involving ion masking). Inspired by these studies, a new method combining the advantages both of pH sensitivity and catalytic dye formation method is introduced.

In this work, based on (EDTA-Ni)-LDH, we developed a new analytical approach, which can be used to detect Cu2+ and Fe3+ together ( Fig. 1). At pH 6.5, Cu2+ can be extracted by (EDTA-Ni)-LDH and released at pH 4.5, with the released Cu2+ catalyzing the polymerization of aniline. Similarly, the extraction pH and release pH for Fe3+ are 4.5 and 6.5, respectively. The ensuing PANI synthesized under different pH values (4.5 for Cu2+ and 6.5 for Fe3+) show individual UV absorption properties, which can be applied to measure the concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+, respectively. Thus, in the same process, dual-selectivity is enabled. The Cu2+ and Fe3+ participate in the polymerization of PANI as catalysts, leading to amplified detection signals and improved sensitivity. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the process of polymerization of aniline is taken advantage of, for the detection of metal ions.

Chromium (II) nitrate nonahydrate, manganese nitrate tetrahydrate, nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O) and magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (Mg(NO3)2·6H2O) were purchased from Alfa Aesar (Heysham, UK). Zinc nitrate tetrahydrate was obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and cobalt (II) nitrate hexahydrate was purchased from Fisher Scientific (Loughborough, UK). Iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate, iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate, potassium nitrate, sodium nitrate, silver nitrate, mercury chloride, copper nitrate trihydrate, calcium nitrate tetrahydrate, sodium acetate anhydrate (99%), aniline (> 99.5%), polyacrylic acid (2100 sodium salt) (PAA) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Hydrogen peroxide (30–32%) was procured from Qiangsheng Chemical (Jiangsu，China). All standard solutions were prepared by dissolving the required amount of reagents with deionized water, and thoroughly mixed, before use. All metal salts were of analytical-reagent grade. Real water samples were collected locally. A 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PB) was used and the pH was adjusted by adding appropriate amount of NaOH and H3PO4 solution

Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) measurements were carried out using a Nanodrop 2000C microvolume spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The reaction solutions were scanned at wavelengths of between 300 and 800 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were made on a Siemens (Karlsruhe, Germany) D5005 X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα = 1.5418 Å). Transmission electron micrographic (TEM) images were taken using a JEOL JEM-3010 TEM (Tokyo, Japan) instrument. Scanning electron micrography (SEM) was conducted using a JEOL JSM-6701F SEM system (Tokyo, Japan). An Agilent Technologies Model 3200P pH meter (Palo Alto, CA, USA) was used to adjust the pH of buffer solutions. ICP-MS was performed on an Agilent Technologies Model 7500 CX system.

The sorbent was synthesized by a co-precipitation method, and subsequently hydrothermally-treated according to a previous report [34]. First, 14.5 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water, and the resulting solution was added in a dropwise manner to 50 mL of a 0.2 M EDTA aqueous solution. The obtained deep blue solution of (EDTA-Ni)2- was stirred vigorously at 65 °C. The second step involved dissolving 7.68 g of Mg(NO3)2·6H2O and 3.75 g of Al(NO3)3·9H2O with deionized water to form a 100 mL solution, which was then transferred to the (EDTA-Ni)2- solution by dropwise addition. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 11 by using a 0.1 M NaOH solution. The pale blue solution was stirred at 65 °C for 1 h, followed by aging for 18 h without stirring at the same temperature. Afterwards, the resultant slurry was separated by filtration, washed with deionized water three times, and then dried at 80 °C for 16 h. The final product (ca. 5 g) could be stably stored for later use.

First, 30 mg (EDTA-Ni)-LDH was dispersed in 1 mL of deionized water and placed under ultrasonication for 30 min to prepare a suspension. For each extraction, 100 μL of suspension and 1 mL of sample solution were added to a centrifuge tube (2 mL capacity). Next, the tube was placed in a water bath set at the designated temperature for a specified time for extraction (pH was adjusted by PB solution). During this process, the tube was vortexed every five minutes to ensure the homogeneous distribution of LDHs. Thereafter, the sorbent was separated from the solution by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min, and the supernatant was decanted. Finally, the (EDTA-Cu or Fe)-LDH was obtained.

Two hundred microliters of 50 mM aniline/PAA solution (pH was adjusted by PB solution) and 20 μL H2O2 were mixed with (EDTA-Cu or Fe)-LDH from the above step. The mixture solution was vortexed for 1 min, and the polymerization reaction was monitored by UV–vis spectrophotometry, and the change in absorbance was determined at 370 nm for Cu2+, and 310 nm for Fe3+.

The XRD spectra of the prepared LDHs are shown in Fig. 2. To compare with (EDTA-Ni)-LDH, the NO3-LDH was also synthesized. Both spectra are characterized by a layered phase with the basal diffractions, and these planes are marked by (003), (006), (009). For (EDTA-Ni)-LDH, three symmetric peaks precisely measured at 2θ 6.0°, 12.10° and 18.36° demonstrated basal spacings d of 14.7, 7.3, 4.8 Å, and for NO3-LDH, three peaks at 2θ 11.3°, 22.80°, 33.44° revealed d values of 7.8, 3.9, 2.6 Å. These results indicated that both NO3-LDH and (EDTA-Ni)-LDH had layered structures. Evidently, (EDTA-Ni)-LDH has some particular peaks that differ from NO3-LDH, and the intercalation of (EDTA-Ni)2- in LDH results in an increase in basal spacing from 7.4 Å to 14.7 Å as well. In addition, subtraction of the average hydrotalcite-layer thickness of 4.8 Å from d = 14.7 Å, gave 9.9 Å, which is the interlayer distance due to the intercalation of (EDTA-Ni)2-. On the basis of the above results, the (EDTA-Ni)-LDH was deemed to be synthesized successfully. The TEM image of (EDTA-Ni)-LDH is shown in Fig. 2 (insert). From the image, it can be observed that the LDH sheets have an approximate size of 20–100 nm.

In this analytical process, pH is an important factor since it affects (1) the extraction and release of metal ions from the sorbents, and (2) the structures of the polymerization products. With increase of pH, the formation constants of (EDTA-Cu)2- are raised and are always higher than that of (EDTA-Ni)2-; thus, Cu2+ can be exchanged with Ni2+. The formation constant reached a maximum at pH 9. However, Cu2+ begins to precipitate as Cu(OH)2 when pH > 6.5 at the concentration of our experiments (≤ 5 μM). In addition, the decreasing pH enhances the solubility of Cu2+ and increases its affinity for the aqueous phase. Thus the low pH weakens the extraction of Cu2+. To achieve a higher extraction efficiency and avoid the interference of Cu(OH)2, a pH of 6 was selected as the most favorable pH for extraction. Similarly, a pH of 4.5 was chosen as the extraction pH for Fe3+. Unlike Cu2+, the formation constant of (EDTA-Fe)- decreases when pH > 5.

In the second step (release and catalysis), for a suitable reaction pH, these aspects need to be considered: (1) To promote the release of Cu2+ into the reaction solution, a lower pH is required (smaller formation constant and higher solubility); on the contrary, a higher pH promotes the release of Fe3+; and (2) When pH < 4, (EDTA-Ni)-LDH would dissolve [35] and release another main interfering cation (Ni2+); and (3) The effect on the polymerization of aniline. As can be seen from Fig. 3, as the reaction pH decreases, the peak intensity at 370 nm increases, which is due to the linear-chain polyaniline (Fig. 3 insert). When reaction pH is 4.5, the signal of Cu2+ reaches the maximum. Thus, pH 4.5 was chosen as the most favorable reaction pH, and 370 nm was applied as the detection wavelength. It also can be seen ( Fig. 4), that the ring structure dominates in the PANI and the highest intensity of Fe3+ is obtained at pH 6.5 (Fig. 4 insert). The appropriate wavelength is 310 nm. On the basis of the above discussion, a pH of 4.5 was adopted for the reaction solution of Cu2+ detection, and 6.5 for that of Fe3+.

Extraction times (of between 5 and 45 min) and extraction temperatures (of between 25 and 55 °C) were studied for both the Cu2+ and Fe3+ extraction processes, and the results are shown in Figs. S-2 and S-3. It can be seen that the output signal intensities were enhanced with increasing temperature and time. However, the extraction is equilibrium-based, and absorbance is at a maximum when the equilibrium condition is established. The extraction profile finally flattens out, indicating that equilibrium has been attained (grey region in Figs. S-2 and S-3). The (EDTA-M)2- complex is highly stable and does not dissociate over time. According to the results of Figs. S-2 and S-3, 35 °C and 25 min were chosen as the optimized extraction temperature and time for Cu2+, respectively. As for Fe3+, 35 °C and 35 min were selected as the most favorable extraction conditions.

The effect of reaction time on the intensity of the absorbance was evaluated. For the polymerization of aniline catalyzed by Cu2+ at pH 4.5, the rate of polymerization is faster than that catalyzed by Fe3+ at pH 6.5. Taking the case of Fe3+ as an example, It can be seen from Fig. 5 that after 120 min, the curve becomes flat, indicating that the reaction is complete. However, detection at the time of reaction completion was not sensitive for various concentrations of catalyst, which limited the detection linearity range. Moreover, the reaction time was too long for practical purposes. After 50 min, the curve deviated from linearity, indicating that the reaction rate had begun to slow down. Thus, a reaction time of 50 min was a better choice for a rapid detection for the case of Fe3+, across a wider linearity range. Based on a similar reason, 5 min was adopted as a favorable reaction time for Cu2+.

The UV–vis intensity (Δabsorbance) of polymerization of aniline reaction catalyzed by (1) Cu2+/Fe3+ standard solution (without extraction) and (2) (EDTA-Cu)-LDH ((EDTA-Ni)-LDH after extraction of Cu2+/Fe3+ standard solution) are depicted respectively in Fig. 6. It is obvious that (EDTA-Cu2+/Fe3+)-LDH has a stronger absorbance than the standard solution, and the ΔAbsorbance increased by nearly 4 times. The reason is the Cu2+/Fe3+ was enriched by (EDTA-Ni)-LDH during the extraction process, thus greatly improving the catalytic ability after Cu2+/Fe3+ were released. However, since there existed an equilibrium regime between the Cu2+/Fe3+ and (EDTA-Cu2+/Fe3+)-LDH, the Cu2+ was not released completely (theoretical enrichment factor is ca. 5 times). Nevertheless, the detection signals were amplified from than that in the original solution. This also demonstrated that release of Cu2+/Fe3+ was successful.

To evaluate the selectivity of the method, common metal cations including Na+, K+, Ag+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cr2+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ (whose solutions were prepared using the corresponding compounds listed in the Experimental section) were examined. The influence of these interfering cations was insignificant, indicating the selective extraction and release process eliminated successfully the influence of these other cations (
Figs. 7 and 8). The main reason is these metal cations have different formation constants of the chelated complexes with EDTA (Fe3+ >> Cu2+ > Ni2+ > Hg2+ > Zn2+ > Co2+ > Al3+ >> Fe2+ > Mn2+ > Cr2+ >> Ca2+ > Mg2+ > Ag+ (Table S-1), or they cannot chelate with EDTA), like Na+ and K+. During the extraction step, ion-exchange between Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ni2+ was dominant, since Fe3+ and Cu2+ have stronger formation constants than Ni2+. Thus, other metal cations could not be extracted efficiently. Then during the release process, when the pH was changed, the formation constants of Cu2+ or Fe3+ decreased [36,37]. The chelation between metal ions and EDTA weakened, making it more facile for the metal ions to be released into the reaction solution. Therefore, as expected, polymerization of aniline was only catalyzed by the released metal ions. All these observations indicate that the method has significant selectivity for Cu2+ and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method evaluation and genuine sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the performance of the method for determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+, the linear ranges and the LODs were measured under the most favorable experimental conditions. The linear concentration range of Cu2+ was determined to be between 0.0005 µM and 2.5 µM ( Fig. 9) with coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.9904. The LOD, based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, was measured to be 0.1 nM (6.4 ng/L). The relative standard deviation (RSD %), was 2.5%, calculated by applying the proposed method to three replicate sample solutions containing 100 nM of Cu2+. As for Fe3+, the linear range was between 0.005 µM and 5 µM with r2 = 0.9965. The LOD was 1 nM (56 ng/L) and the RSD % was 3.2% ( Fig. 10).

A comparison of the present results with those of other methods is shown in Table 1. As can be seen, the LODs of this proposed method were lower than most other methods. Meanwhile, wide working ranges were obtained in this work. Essentially, in the other detection methods, the signals were produced by the combination of sensors and metal ions. Thus, the reaction was usually first order, in which the metal ions acted as reactants. In the present work, the metal ions participated in the polymerization reaction as catalysts. Therefore, the signals were amplified significantly. Another important difference is that in most of the other methods [14–16,35,36], the detection wavelengths for Fe3+ and Cu2+ were the same. To distinguish the signals of Fe3+ and Cu2+, a further reaction was usually required [38–40]. In contrast, the respective detection wavelengths were different from each other in this work, which further reduced possible interferences. Some methods detected different signals, either using different types of detection instruments (UV–vis and florescence) [41,42] or the signals produced by a series of materials [43]. However, in this method the different UV–vis signals were generated in the same process, and based on (EDTA-Ni)LDH only.

To evaluate the applicability of the method, the determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in the river water samples was carried out, and the results are compared to those from ICP-MS analysis. Cu2+ and Fe3+ at levels of 152.1 nM, 71.4 nM, respectively, were detected in the river water. The values from ICP-MS determination was 148.3 nM and 68.3 nM, correspondingly ( Table 2). These genuine samples were also spiked at 50 and 1000 nM of metal ions, respectively, and subjected to the procedure. As shown in Table 2, good recoveries (94.6–99.4%) were obtained for the spiked samples; the results were comparable with those obtained by ICP-MS.

In this study, a pH-dependent extraction-release-catalysis detection process was introduced to determine trace levels of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous samples. The selectivity of the method was achieved based on the following: (1) The metal ions were selectively exchanged with Ni2+ in the extraction step, (2) The metal ions were easily released from the sorbent, and (3) The different structures of polyanilines with separated signals were produced under different pH values. Through two steps, (1) Extraction of the metal ions by the sorbent for enrichment, and (2) Catalysis by the metal ions, the detection signal was amplified. This procedure demonstrated a highly sensitive and selective method for the determination of Cu2+ and Fe3+ together in water samples. The characteristics of aniline polymerization can be further exploited for other applications in colorimetric detection. We believe that by using chelating agents and chelated ions other than EDTA and Ni2+, this method could be applied to detect other metal cations. Some ions (e.g., S2-), can be directly extracted by LDH without chelating agents, and so can serve as potential analytes in future applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, National University of Singapore, National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21705060, 21605031, 21605105), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (No. BK20170570) and College Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (No. 17KJB150014) and Six Talent Peaks Project in Jiangsu Province, China (No. SWYY-021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.04.102.
